board.verdict: We have a beautiful, well-built dashboard that quietly fails its own headline promise. I verified it in the code, not just the briefs: cold-start (coldStart.ts + WelcomeColdStart.tsx) still seeds FAKE tasks ('Read this week's lecture notes', 'Revise for the upcoming quiz') that Maya must delete, and never once offers to import her real timetable even though AddFeedForm + the webcal->https rewrite already exist. deadlineEntries() in widgets/util.ts is topic-only and provably blind to imported .ics exams/coursework. There is no first-class deadline object: types.ts has no recurrence on TopicItem, no topicId on transactions, no TimeEntry, so a brand-new account with zero topics hits a literal dead end. The keyboard layer is exactly two shortcuts (Dashboard.tsx:608-618). The 'morning in 90 seconds' north star is delivered only as a passive widget on a self-built grid with no day-2 guidance. The machinery is genuinely strong (offline NLP parser, Worlds, RRULE expansion in ical.ts, cold-start seeding) but it is wired up to demo well, not to get a real student's real life in, in 90 seconds. The gap between 'this is a slick demo' and 'this gets me' is the whole game, and right now we are on the wrong side of it. This round we close that gap, hard. I do NOT sign off this round; the data spine and the first-run spine both need to land before I'm comfortable.
topPriorities: SPINE 1 - Real timetable IS onboarding. Rip out the fake sample tasks. After Maya taps 'Uni' in WelcomeColdStart, the very next screen is 'Paste your timetable link' (reuse AddFeedForm + the existing webcal->https rewrite + a one-line 'where do I find this?' helper for common UK uni / Google Calendar). Her real parsed classes fill Today and the calendar in 90 seconds. This is the single highest-leverage change: it converts first-run from 'fake demo' to 'this gets me' and is the literal north star made real.

SPINE 2 - One unified 'what's due' stream across feed AND tasks. Make deadlineEntries() (and the Today widget) also ingest calendar events that are deadline-like (all-day, or title matches due/deadline/exam/submission/coursework), so the headline Deadlines widget stops being blind to her imported exams. One ranked next-7-days list answers 'what's due' across her timetable feed and her own tasks - one question, one answer, no cross-checking two screens. Pairs directly with Spine 1: the import is worthless if the deadlines widget can't see it.

SPINE 3 - A first-class, top-level deadline you can capture with no topic. Introduce a lightweight global 'add a deadline' (date + optional time + optional topic) reachable from Quick-add and the Today/Deadlines widgets that works with ZERO topics, defaulting to an auto 'Unfiled'/'Uni' bucket. The taskParser already understands 'friday 5pm', so 'stats essay due friday 5pm' becomes a real dated deadline in one line. This kills the new-account dead end (Quicks empty-state telling her 'no topics yet') and makes capture honest about the app's whole reason to exist: deadlines.

SPINE 4 - The active 'Morning in 90 seconds' ritual, not a passive widget. A hero/full-screen 'Good morning' flow that auto-runs first-open each day reusing priorities() and dailyPlan: (1) here's what's due / next event, (2) tap the 3 things you'll actually do, (3) anything new on your mind (inline capture), then 'you're set' into Home. This finally makes the headline promise a repeatable flow with closure instead of a silent grid the day after onboarding ends. Lower mechanical risk than the data work, so it's the 4th not the 1st, but it is what makes her TRUST it enough to come back tomorrow.

board.role: CPO (Product) — activation, retention, the core daily loop
board.verdict:
The engine is more impressive than the experience. The hard parts are already built and genuinely good: TodayWidget (src/components/widgets/TodayWidget.tsx) is a real narrated morning brief, the ICS importer with webcal rewrite (calendar/AddFeedForm.tsx) works, the offline NLP parser routes and dates tasks, and cold-start (lib/coldStart.ts) seeds a populated board in one tap. But the daily loop our north star promises ("your morning in 90 seconds") is NOT delivered, because three seams are unconnected — and I confirmed all three in code.

(1) ACTIVATION LIES ABOUT WHAT BOZZ IS. The single thing a student comes for — her real timetable — is never asked for at first run. WelcomeColdStart's "Uni" tap seeds FAKE tasks she must delete (coldStart.ts:53-57: "Read this week's lecture notes", "Revise for the upcoming quiz") and the only real importer is buried inside the Calendar section. The CONNECT walkthrough (Onboarding.tsx:140-143) never even mentions calendar. First impression = fake demo, not "this gets me."

(2) THE HEADLINE "WHAT'S DUE" SURFACE IS BLIND TO HER CALENDAR. deadlineEntries() in widgets/util.ts ingests ONLY topic items. feedEvents flow into todayEvents and the grid (Dashboard.tsx:775-782) but NEVER into priorities() or Upcoming deadlines. So an imported exam/coursework date literally cannot appear in the widget called "deadlines." The bridge is one-way (topicDeadlineEvents pushes topics→calendar; nothing reverse). One question, two answers, in two screens.

(3) THERE IS NO LOOP AFTER DAY 1. dismissOnboarding (Dashboard.tsx:371-376) drops a returning user onto a silent grid with zero "what now." No morning ritual, no coach, no triage nudge. And a brand-new account can't even log a deadline without first owning a topic — Quicks dead-ends at "no topics yet" (InboxView.tsx:211-218). Capture is frictionless; everything after it leaks.

We have a 9/10 engine wired into a 5/10 loop. This round is about connecting what already exists, not building new pillars. I am deliberately deprioritising the entire freelancer/client-cockpit/time-tracking/invoicing track this round — it's a different persona and a land-grab, not the activation+retention fix our primary user (students) needs first.
topPriorities: 1) MAKE THE REAL TIMETABLE THE SPINE OF ONBOARDING. After the cold-start 'Uni/Work' tap, the very next screen is 'Paste your timetable link' — reuse AddFeedForm.tsx (webcal->https rewrite + fetch validation already exist) with a one-line 'where do I find this?' helper. Replace the fake sample tasks in coldStart.ts:53-57 with her real parsed classes filling Today and the calendar in 90 seconds. This is the difference between 'fake demo' and 'this gets me.' (boldness 4, highest-leverage activation fix)

2) UNIFY 'WHAT'S DUE' INTO ONE STREAM. Make deadlineEntries() (widgets/util.ts) and the Today/Upcoming-deadlines widgets also ingest deadline-like calendar events (all-day events, or titles matching exam/due/deadline/submission/coursework) so ONE ranked next-7-days list answers 'what's due' across her imported timetable AND her own tasks. Pure derivation over feedEvents already in Dashboard.tsx:766-782 — closes the most glaring contradiction in the product (a 'deadlines' widget that can't see her exam dates).

3) SHIP A FIRST-CLASS, TOPIC-FREE DEADLINE CAPTURE + KILL THE DEAD ENDS. Let her log 'stats essay due friday 5pm' from Quick add and the Today/Deadlines widgets with NO topic selected (auto 'Unfiled'/cold-start bucket). The taskParser already extracts the date for free. Fix the Quicks 'no topics yet' wall (InboxView.tsx:211-218) with inline 'new topic from this Quick.' Removes the structural reason a new account stalls.

4) GIVE THE LOOP A DAY-2 HEARTBEAT: a small, dismissible, state-aware 'next step' coach chip on Home (one ranked suggestion: 'Add your timetable ->', 'X quicks to sort ->', 'Plan your day ->', 'Connect your inbox ->'). It reads signals already computed in Dashboard (calendarFeeds, inbox, topics, dailyPlan, todayEvents) and routes via setActiveSection. Replaces the silent grid after dismissOnboarding (Dashboard.tsx:371-376) with a permanent low-pressure handrail — the single biggest retention gap for a non-techy user. (boldness 4)

board.verdict: The foundation is genuinely strong: the offline NLP parser (taskParser.ts) is a real, defensible moat (multi-task split, deadline NLP, topic prediction) with zero token cost; cold-start seeding works; TodayWidget already narrates priorities+events+plan; and the central handlers (addTopicItem, onAdvanceStage, todayEvents memo, deadlineEntries) are clean enough that bold features can be pure derivations with low regression risk. TS build is `tsc && vite build` and is green. BUT three structural gaps undercut the north star and read as correctness debt, not missing polish. (1) The deadline model is a one-way valve: deadlineEntries() (widgets/util.ts:11) ingests ONLY topic items, while topicDeadlineEvents() pushes topics->calendar and never the reverse, so a student's imported .ics exams/coursework are INVISIBLE to the headline 'Upcoming deadlines' widget. (2) A deadline can only exist as a TopicItem requiring a topicId (Dashboard addTopicItem:698), so a brand-new zero-topic account hits a hard dead end -- InboxView.tsx:217 literally tells the user 'you have items but no topics yet'. (3) There is zero cross-topic actionable aggregation (tasks live only in per-topic widgets) and search (search.ts:25) indexes budget/inbox/feed/9 labels but NOT a single topic item, name, link or note -- the largest body of content is unsearchable and Cmd-K can only navigate, not act. My round-1 priorities deliberately favour model-level fixes that make every downstream widget correct for free, are self-contained React/TS derivations over existing setters, and keep TS green -- not net-new pillars (time-tracking, share pages, client cockpit) which are bolder product bets but higher surface area and better sequenced after the model is sound.
topPriorities: Unify the 'what's due' stream (boldness 4): make deadlineEntries() in widgets/util.ts ALSO ingest calendar events that are deadline-like (all-day events, or titles matching due/deadline/exam/submission/coursework) so one ranked next-7-days list answers 'what's due' across BOTH the imported .ics timetable and the user's own tasks. This is a correctness fix at the source (util.ts) -- TodayWidget priorities() and UpcomingDeadlinesWidget both consume it, so both get fixed for free. Self-contained, no schema/auth touched, TS-safe.

Introduce a first-class top-level deadline capture that works with NO topic (boldness 4): add a lightweight global 'X is due Y' surface reachable from Quick add and the Today/Deadlines widgets that defaults to an auto 'Unfiled'/'Uni' bucket when no topic is selected, killing the zero-topic dead end (InboxView.tsx:217). Reuse the existing extractDeadline() so 'essay due friday 5pm' becomes a real dated deadline in one line. Pairs directly with priority #1 -- a captured deadline must have somewhere correct to land.

Ship a cross-topic 'Focus / This week' actionable view + home widget (boldness 5): flatten every topic's items into one keyboard-navigable list grouped Overdue / Today / This week / No date, with topic chips and INLINE complete + stage-advance (reusing onAdvanceStage/addTopicItem already in Dashboard). This is the literal 'one place for everything due' the north star promises and is pure derivation over the topics array -- no new model, low regression risk. Build it to consume the unified stream from #1 so calendar deadlines appear here too.

Make Cmd-K a real command palette (boldness 4): extend buildSearchIndex (search.ts) to index every TopicItem (with topic+stage+deadline), topic names, links and notes, and let results carry inline actions (complete task, file Quick, jump to topic) executed with Enter instead of only onJump(section). Self-contained over existing Dashboard setters, no backend. Highest-leverage power-user fix and it compounds with #3 since both are views over the same flattened task data.

board.verdict:
Where we are: Rounds 1-2 fixed the right things first. The cold-start front door (WelcomeColdStart -> seedColdStart) means a brand-new account now lands on a populated, colour-coded, predictable dashboard instead of empty shells, and the .ics importer (AddFeedForm, the only setCalendarFeeds caller) finally has UI. The Today widget became a real morning brief that auto-merges due/overdue tasks. That is genuine progress against the north star and it shows operational discipline: we built the door before decorating the rooms. BUT the spine is still bent in three places, and they are all the same wound -- the app has TWO planning worlds that do not talk to each other.

1) The 90-second promise leaks at the seam. Cold-start seeds FAKE tasks Maya must delete ("Revise for the upcoming quiz" due in 6 days), and after tapping "Uni" the very next screen is NOT "paste your timetable." The importer exists but is buried in Calendar. So her ONE reason for coming (her real timetable in one place) is the one thing onboarding never asks for. We shipped the importer and the cold-start in the same two rounds and STILL did not connect them. That is the single highest-leverage fix and it is now cheap because both halves already exist.

2) "Deadlines" is structurally blind. deadlineEntries() in widgets/util.ts ingests ONLY topic items. topicDeadlineEvents() pushes topic deadlines ONTO the calendar but never the reverse. So the headline "Upcoming deadlines" widget literally cannot show an exam or submission date that arrived via her imported feed -- exactly the hard deadlines a student cares most about. One question ("what's due"), two answers she has to cross-check. Unacceptable for a deadline app.

3) There is no first-class deadline object and no cross-topic actionable list. A deadline can only exist as a TopicItem bolted to a topic she has to own first; a zero-topic account hits the "no topics yet" wall. And to see what's due across topics she must open each one. The planner is 4 rolling columns; the week grid is a separate screen. Three things she asked to be in one place live in three places.

On scope: we are NOT spread too thin yet -- the breadth (map, linkedin, whatsapp, notion) is fine as long as the student spine is sharp, and right now it isn't. The freelancer/Client-Cockpit cluster (ClientBoard, billable time tracking, per-client money, shareable status page) is a coherent, attractive SECOND act, but it is a different ICP and a different go-to-market. We do NOT open that front until the student core is undeniable. Cut it from this round. Same for the export/import World marketplace and the command-palette overhaul -- good, but premium polish on a spine that still has a gap in it.

On monetisation: Plus is fully beta-unlocked (plus.ts BETA_UNLOCK=true) and every feature ships free, which is correct for now -- we are buying signal, not revenue. None of my priorities touch the paywall, billing, or entitlement, so the hard constraints hold. The thing that will eventually justify Plus is depth on this exact spine (a term's worth of recurring structure, the week view, trustworthy aggregation), so fixing the core IS the monetisation work.
topPriorities: P1 -- Chain timetable paste into cold-start (the front door). After 'Uni' in WelcomeColdStart, the very next screen is 'Paste your timetable link' reusing AddFeedForm + the existing webcal->https rewrite, with a one-line 'where do I find this?' helper. Replace the fake seeded sample tasks with her REAL parsed classes filling Today and the calendar. Both halves already exist (rounds 1-2 shipped them separately) -- this is connection, not construction, so it is the highest leverage / lowest cost change we have. This is the difference between 'this gets me' and 'fake demo.'

P2 -- Unify the 'what's due' stream so deadlines stop being blind. Make deadlineEntries() (and Today/Upcoming) also ingest calendar events that are deadline-like (all-day, or title matches due/deadline/exam/submission/coursework) so ONE ranked next-7-days list answers 'what's due' across BOTH her imported feed and her own tasks. Pure derivation over existing state. This is the credibility fix: a deadline app that can't see her exam dates is broken.

P3 -- First-class top-level deadline capture that works with NO topic. A lightweight 'X is due Y' surface from Quick-add and the Today/Deadlines widgets that defaults to an auto 'Uni'/'Unfiled' bucket when no topic is selected, reusing the existing extractDeadline parser ('essay due friday 5pm' -> dated deadline in one line). Kills the 'no topics yet' dead-end and the topic-plumbing tax on the single most common action.

P4 (stretch, only if P1-P3 land clean) -- A real 'This week' Mon-Sun view that overlays imported class blocks WITH tasks/deadlines per day, composing the existing CalendarView time-grid + DailyPlannerView assignment. This is the literal 'timetable + deadlines + tasks in one place' she asked for. If time is tight, defer to next round rather than ship it half-built -- P1-P3 are the non-negotiables.

opportunities.title: Make 'import your real timetable' the spine of onboarding, not fake sample tasks
opportunities.area: Onboarding / cold-start + calendar feed (WelcomeColdStart.tsx, lib/coldStart.ts, Onboarding.tsx CONNECT_STEPS, AddFeedForm.tsx)
opportunities.rationale: Maya came for one thing: her timetable in one place. But first-run never asks for it. WelcomeColdStart's 'Uni' choice (coldStart.ts COLD_START_OPTIONS) seeds FAKE tasks ('Read this week's lecture notes' due tomorrow, 'Revise for the upcoming quiz' due in 6 days) that she has to delete, and a 'student' home layout. The actual timetable importer (AddFeedForm.tsx, the only caller of setCalendarFeeds, headlined 'Add your timetable') is buried inside the Calendar section. The CONNECT walkthrough (Onboarding.tsx:140-143) only says 'connect Gmail' and never mentions calendar at all. Bold fix: after she taps 'Uni' in cold-start, the very next screen is 'Paste your timetable link' (reuse AddFeedForm + the webcal-> https rewrite that already exists), with a one-line 'where do I find this?' helper per common UK uni/Google Calendar. Replace the dead sample tasks with her real, parsed classes filling Today and the calendar in 90 seconds. This is the difference between 'this gets me' and 'this is a fake demo.'

opportunities.title: Unify calendar events and topic deadlines into one 'what's due' stream
opportunities.area: Deadline aggregation (widgets/util.ts deadlineEntries, lib/calendar.ts, UpcomingDeadlinesWidget, TodayScheduleWidget)
opportunities.rationale: There are two disconnected planning worlds that never talk. deadlineEntries() in widgets/util.ts pulls ONLY topic items, so the 'Upcoming deadlines' widget literally cannot show an exam or coursework date that came in from her imported .ics timetable. Meanwhile topicDeadlineEvents() pushes topic deadlines onto the calendar, but never the reverse. For a student whose hard deadlines (exams, submission dates) arrive via the university calendar feed, the headline 'deadlines' widget is blind to them. Bold change: make deadlineEntries (and the Today widget) also ingest calendar events tagged as deadline-like (all-day events, or events whose title matches due/deadline/exam/submission/coursework), so one ranked 'next 7 days' list answers 'what's due' across BOTH her timetable feed and her own tasks. One question, one answer, instead of her cross-checking calendar vs deadlines.

opportunities.title: A global 'add a deadline' surface that isn't trapped inside a topic
opportunities.area: Data model + capture (Dashboard.addTopicItem, QuickAddModal, DailyPlannerView, types TopicItem)
opportunities.rationale: Today a deadline can only exist as a TopicItem bolted onto a topic (Dashboard.tsx addTopicItem). To log 'stats assignment due Friday 5pm,' Maya must (1) own a topic, (2) add an item to it, (3) set its deadline. A brand-new account has zero topics, so the Quicks empty-state literally tells her 'you have items but no topics yet' (InboxView.tsx:217) and she's stuck. There's no first-class 'deadline' object and no single place to just say 'X is due Y.' Bold move: introduce a lightweight top-level deadline capture (date + optional time + optional topic), reachable from Quick add and the Today/Deadlines widgets, that works with NO topic selected (defaults to an auto 'Uni' or 'Unfiled' bucket). The natural-language date parser (taskParser extractDeadline) already understands 'Friday', 'next week', 'June 15' for free, so 'essay due friday 5pm' should become a real dated deadline in one line without topic plumbing.

opportunities.title: Recurring deadlines/tasks for the weekly rhythm of a course
opportunities.area: Task model + planner (types TopicItem, DailyPlannerView, lib/calendar.ts; mirror the RRULE expansion already in lib/ical.ts)
opportunities.rationale: A student's week is overwhelmingly recurring: 'tutorial prep every Tuesday', 'lab report each Friday', 'reading before Thursday's lecture'. ICS feeds expand RRULE recurrence (lib/ical.ts iterator + getOccurrenceDetails) so her CLASSES recur correctly, but her own tasks/deadlines have none, every TopicItem is a one-off. So she re-types the same task weekly, or it silently disappears after she ticks it once. Bold: add a recurrence field to deadline/task items (weekly/by-weekday is enough for 90% of student life) and expand them into the planner and the Today/Deadlines stream the same bounded way ical.ts already does for events. This turns Bozz from a one-shot todo list into something that actually models a term's cadence, the thing a paper planner does that her current setup can't.

opportunities.title: One-tap triage so capturing 5 things doesn't cost 15 clicks
opportunities.area: Quicks triage flow (InboxView.tsx, QuickAddModal addAll/addLater, taskParser.predictTopic)
opportunities.rationale: Maya is impatient and judges fast. The capture model is good (ramble -> parsed cards, predicted topic, 'Accept all predicted'), but the everyday loop still has friction: every Quick that ISN'T confidently predicted needs her to open a ChoicePicker, pick a topic, open a DatePicker, set a date, then hit send, per row (InboxView InboxRow). predictTopic only fires above a keyword-score threshold, so early on (few topics, sparse keywords) most items fall through to manual. Bold: make triage feel like Tinder/Superhuman, full-keyboard or swipe flow where each Quick shows the single best topic+date guess big, and one key/tap = accept, one = skip, with the date inferred from the text she already typed. Plus an inline 'new topic from this Quick' so she's never blocked by the 'no topics yet' wall. Capture is already fast; the sorting is where she'll bounce.

opportunities.title: A real 'this week' timetable view, the mental model students actually use
opportunities.area: Planner (DailyPlannerView is 4 generic day columns; CalendarView week grid exists but is separate)
opportunities.rationale: Students live in weeks, Monday-to-Sunday with classes laid on a time grid, not in a rolling '4 day columns starting today' window (DailyPlannerView DAY_COUNT=4, windowStart). The pieces exist but are split: CalendarView has a proper time-grid week (GRID_START_HOUR/HOUR_PX) for events, DailyPlannerView has task-to-day assignment, and neither shows classes + tasks + deadlines together in the one weekly shape she recognises from a paper timetable. Bold: build a single 'This week' view that overlays her imported class blocks (timed ical events) WITH her tasks/deadlines for each day, Mon-Sun, so she sees 'I have a 9am lecture, then this essay is due, then free until 2' at a glance. That is the literal 'one place for deadlines, timetable, tasks' she asked for, and right now those three things live in three different screens.

opportunities.title: Client Cockpit: a per-client status board on Home
opportunities.area: Home / widgets (new ClientBoardWidget) + topic-folder model
opportunities.rationale: As Tom I juggle 4 clients and every client is just a topic (or folder of topics), but the app gives me no single 'where does each client stand' view. SummaryWidget (src/components/widgets/SummaryWidget.tsx) only sums doing/todo/done globally; TodayWidget (src/components/widgets/TodayWidget.tsx) is time-bucketed, not client-segmented; UpcomingDeadlines mixes everyone together. I open 4 topics one by one to reconstruct status. BOLD fix: a new ClientBoardWidget that renders one lane per chosen topic/folder, each showing next deadline + urgency, open-vs-done task count, a 'waiting on them' flag, last activity, and (if connected) unread email count from that client's domain. Data already exists - topics, stages, deadlineEntries() in widgets/util.ts, ctx.emails - it just needs to be pivoted by client instead of by time. This is the at-a-glance status screen a tab-living freelancer actually wants, and it leans into the 'morning in 90 seconds' north star for the multi-client case.

opportunities.title: Billable time tracking tied to topics (turn Pomodoro into a real timesheet)
opportunities.area: New TimeTrack lib + widget; rework PomodoroWidget; per-topic time log in types.ts
opportunities.rationale: PomodoroWidget (src/components/widgets/PomodoroWidget.tsx) is a generic focus timer: it isn't attached to any topic/client, the count resets every session, and nothing is ever logged. For a freelancer this is a huge miss - I bill hours and currently the app records zero of where my time went. BOLD proposal: a topic-aware time tracker. Start/stop a timer (or auto-log completed Pomodoros) against the current topic, persist sessions as a per-topic time log (new TimeEntry[] alongside TopicItem in types.ts), and surface 'X hrs this week' per client on the topic page and in the Client Cockpit above. Optional hourly rate per topic turns logged hours into an amount. This is a whole new pillar (time -> money) and is self-contained React/TS with local storage, no auth/payments touched.

opportunities.title: Per-client money: link 'owed-to-me' to a topic and show invoice status
opportunities.area: Budget model (types.ts OneOffTransaction) + BudgetWidget + topic page
opportunities.rationale: BudgetData already has the 'owed-to-me' / 'i-owe' TransactionType (src/lib/types.ts) but nothing connects an amount to a client topic, and BudgetWidget (src/components/widgets/BudgetWidget.tsx) only shows global net + a savings goal. As Tom my real money question is per-client: 'Acme owes me 1,200, invoiced 3 weeks ago, unpaid.' BOLD fix: add an optional topicId to owed/i-owe transactions, then show an 'Outstanding' strip per client (amount owed, days since invoiced, overdue styling reusing the amber/alert urgency from UpcomingDeadlinesWidget) on the topic page and in the Client Cockpit. Combined with billable hours this closes the freelancer loop: track time -> see what's owed -> chase it. Frontend-only, builds on the existing budget lib.

opportunities.title: Freelancer / Client-hub template (a real client world)
opportunities.area: src/lib/worlds.ts templates + STARTER_TEMPLATES in widgets/registry.tsx
opportunities.rationale: There's a 'Freelancer' starter layout (registry.tsx STARTER_TEMPLATES) and a Worlds catalog with Study, Job Hunt, Shopping, Family, Holiday, Calm, Deep Work - but NO client/freelancer World, which is exactly my use case. The Freelancer starter is also thin: Today + Budget + Clock + QuickAdd, nothing client-shaped. BOLD fix: ship a 'Client' template World that drops a ready-made client page with bespoke stages (Brief / In progress / Awaiting feedback / Delivered / Invoiced / Paid), plus the new ClientBoard, time tracker, outstanding-money strip, pinned brief note, and links (Figma/Drive/contract). Applied per-folder it spins up a per-client hub in one tap. This makes the 4-clients workflow a first-class, one-click setup instead of me hand-building 4 topics.

opportunities.title: Client-name-aware capture routing (Quicks that knows 'Acme')
opportunities.area: src/lib/taskParser.ts predictTopic + Quicks (InboxView.tsx)
opportunities.rationale: predictTopic in taskParser.ts scores against generic DOMAIN_KEYWORDS (music/fitness/finance...) and topic name words. For me every capture is client-shaped - 'send Acme the v2 mockups', 'Nordwind wants the logo by Friday' - and none of those route correctly unless I manually fill the topic.keywords field, which I'll never discover. The deadline NLP is genuinely good (Friday -> date) but the topic prediction is blind to my clients. BOLD fix: auto-derive client aliases from topic/folder names and recent task text (cheap, offline, no token cost - same spirit as the existing parser), boost proper-noun matches, and let me confirm-to-learn from a wrong guess in Quicks so the Accept-all-predicted flow (InboxView.tsx) actually clears my inbox into the right clients. Turns capture from 'capture then manually triage 4 ways' into 'capture, one tap, done'.

opportunities.title: Shareable read-only client status page (the topic_members groundwork has no UI)
opportunities.area: TopicView.tsx + a new share affordance; leverages existing Supabase topic_members
opportunities.rationale: Memory notes a Supabase topic_members table exists (RLS baseline applied) but the frontend has zero share/collaborate UI - TopicView.tsx and the topic model expose no way to share a topic. As a freelancer the single most repetitive thing I do is tell clients 'where are we'. BOLD proposal: a read-only client status link/snapshot generated from a topic - the brief, current stage, next milestone/deadline, delivered items, and outstanding invoice - that I can send so the client self-serves status instead of emailing me. Even a v1 that exports a clean static status snapshot (no new auth flow, respecting the hard constraint of not touching auth/Supabase schema) would be transformative. This is the boldest reframing: Bozz stops being only my private dashboard and becomes my client-facing front desk.

opportunities.title: A daily "Morning in 90 seconds" guided ritual, not a passive widget
opportunities.area: Home / daily flow / onboarding
opportunities.rationale: The north star is "your morning in 90 seconds," but in the code that promise is delivered only as a passive TodayWidget the user has to read on a self-built grid (src/components/widgets/TodayWidget.tsx). There is no guided morning experience and, critically, no day-2 guidance at all: once Onboarding is dismissed (Dashboard.tsx dismissOnboarding / onbDismissed), a returning user lands on a silent grid with no "what now." For Priya, who stalls without a next step, this is the single biggest gap. BOLD proposal: a full-screen (or hero) "Good morning" ritual that auto-runs on first open each day, walking her card-by-card through today: (1) here's what's due / your next event, (2) tap the 3 things you'll actually do today (one-tap into dailyPlan, which already exists), (3) anything new on your mind? (inline capture), then "you're set, here's your day" closing into Home. It reuses existing data (priorities() in TodayWidget, dailyPlan in Dashboard) and finally makes the headline promise an active, repeatable flow with built-in closure, replacing the dead-end after onboarding. An optional end-of-day 20-second check-in (carry over undone, quick win count) doubles the retention surface.

opportunities.title: Autopilot setup: collapse the two onboarding systems into one conversational "tell me about your life" seed
opportunities.area: Onboarding / first run
opportunities.rationale: Right now first-run has TWO disconnected systems. The cold-start (WelcomeColdStart.tsx + lib/coldStart.ts) seeds ONE topic + a populated board from a single tap, which is great. But then the "Getting started" card (Onboarding.tsx) still pushes Priya through 4 manual walkthroughs that teach her to Edit the sidebar, add topics, pick icons, edit stages, configure widgets, exactly the techy toolbox work she'll stall on, and they ignore that she already has a seeded board. BOLD proposal: replace the walkthrough wall with a short conversational seeder that lets her pick MULTIPLE life areas (Uni + Gym + Job hunt) in one screen instead of one, then optionally "add a couple of things you're worried about this week" (free text → parseVoiceTasks already exists in lib/taskParser.ts → real dated tasks in the right topics). She ends with a real, multi-topic, populated dashboard she never had to assemble. Keep the click-by-click walkthroughs only as an opt-in "show me how to customise" for later, not the default first thing a non-techy 19-year-old sees.

opportunities.title: Make voice "just say what's on your mind" the headline capture, not a buried mic button
opportunities.area: Quicks / capture / home
opportunities.rationale: The ramble-to-tasks engine is genuinely strong and 100% local/free (QuickAddModal.tsx onVoicePartial/onVoiceTranscript + parseVoiceTasks splits "call Adrien and buy strings and revise for the quiz Friday" into separate dated, topic-routed cards). But it's hidden behind a small mic icon inside a modal you have to summon (Ctrl+B). For a non-techy student, talking is far lower-friction than learning where the capture flow lives, and it's the most magical thing the app does. BOLD proposal: surface a big, obvious "Hold to talk / say what's on your mind" affordance directly on Home (and as the primary action in the empty/Quicks states), with the live streaming task cards as the hero animation. This turns the app's best, lowest-effort interaction into the front door and gives Priya a single, unmissable next step whenever she opens it.

opportunities.title: Close the capture-to-done loop: nudge triage and auto-surface undated Quicks so nothing rots
opportunities.area: Quicks / Today / triage
opportunities.rationale: Capture is frictionless but the loop leaks for a non-techy user. A Quick with no date sits in the inbox forever: TodayWidget only surfaces tasks with a deadline (priorities() requires dueTimestamp; undated triaged items never appear) and InboxView never nudges. predictTopic (lib/taskParser.ts) is also inert until topics with keywords exist, so early captures get no "predicted" badge and "Accept all predicted" stays empty. Priya captures, then forgets, then the inbox quietly grows, the opposite of "your morning in 90 seconds." BOLD proposal: (1) a persistent, gentle "X quicks to sort" prompt on Home/Today that opens a fast swipe-style triage (left = today, right = a topic, down = someday) instead of the dense per-row dropdown+date+send row that exists now; (2) auto-suggest a date when she files ("today / this week / no rush") so triaged items actually reach the morning view; (3) seed default keywords on cold-start topics (already partly done in coldStart.ts) and extend prediction so the very first captures route somewhere. This makes the inbox a thing that empties itself with one tap each, not a chore.

opportunities.title: A "life templates" gallery that builds a whole multi-topic dashboard in one tap (beyond the 3 home layouts)
opportunities.area: Topics / templates / home
opportunities.rationale: The building blocks for instant, opinionated setups already exist but are scattered and shallow: STARTER_TEMPLATES (registry.tsx) only sets home WIDGET layouts (Student/Freelancer/Essentials), BUNDLED_WORLDS (lib/worlds.ts, used by WorldsView) can seed a single themed topic, and cold-start seeds one topic. Nowhere can Priya get a complete, realistic "Uni student" setup, multiple topics (Uni, Gym, Social, Money) each with sensible stages, pinned links, a couple of example tasks, AND a matching home, in one move. For someone who stalls on configuration, an opinionated "this is what a student's Bozz looks like" she can accept then tweak is far more useful than a blank canvas plus a toolbox. BOLD proposal: a "Start from a life" gallery (Student, Job-hunter, Gym + wellbeing, Side-project) that composes existing pieces, worldToTopic for look, coldStart-style sample tasks, starter home layout, into a full populated dashboard in one tap. It's mostly assembly of machinery that already exists, so it's self-contained and TS-safe, but it transforms the empty-canvas problem into a "pick your life, done" moment.

opportunities.title: Persistent, friendly "next step" coach that never lets Priya hit a dead end
opportunities.area: Global UX / guidance / empty states
opportunities.rationale: The app is full of dead ends for someone who needs to be told the next step. Empty states explain WHAT a thing is (HomeEmptyState, InboxView empty state) but after the first session there is no ongoing nudge: no connected email but you added a Recent-emails widget (it just says "No email accounts connected yet" in HomeView config, no path), no tasks dated so Today is empty, captures piling up, calendar never connected. The walkthroughs are re-runnable but only via Settings/empty-home, she has to know to go look. BOLD proposal: a small, dismissible, context-aware "coach" chip (one suggestion at a time, ranked) that always offers the single most useful next action based on real state, e.g. "Connect your inbox so Today shows your emails →", "You have 4 quicks to sort →", "Add a deadline so this shows up tomorrow →", "Plan your day (takes 30s) →". It reads existing signals already computed in Dashboard (oauthAccounts, inbox, topics, dailyPlan, todayEvents) and routes via setActiveSection. This gives a non-techy user a permanent, low-pressure handrail instead of a one-shot tutorial that ends and leaves her alone.

opportunities.title: Make Cmd-K a true command palette: search every task, then act without leaving it
opportunities.area: Global search / command palette (src/lib/search.ts, src/components/SearchModal.tsx, Dashboard.tsx)
opportunities.rationale: buildSearchIndex only indexes budget transactions, inbox items, calendar feed events, and 9 static settings labels. It does NOT index any topic task, topic name, pinned link, note, or email - the single largest body of content in the app is unsearchable. Worse, SearchModal.choose() can only onJump(section): hitting Enter navigates you to a page and dumps you to hunt manually. For a keyboard-first power user this is the #1 friction point. BOLD fix: extend SearchEntry to index every TopicItem (with topic + stage + deadline), topic names, links, notes, and cached emails, and turn the palette into a real command bar a la Linear/Raycast - results carry actions (complete task, advance stage, set deadline, file Quick to topic X, open link, jump to topic) executed inline with Enter, plus a '>' command mode ('> new task', '> go calendar', '> connect gmail'). This is self-contained React/TS over existing state setters already living in Dashboard, no backend touched.

opportunities.title: Add a global keyboard layer: nav chords, instant task add, and arrow-driven Quicks triage
opportunities.area: Keyboard shortcuts (Dashboard.tsx onKey handler lines 605-622; InboxView.tsx; SearchModal.tsx)
opportunities.rationale: The entire app exposes exactly two shortcuts: Cmd+K (search) and Cmd+B (capture). There is no way to navigate, create, or triage from the keyboard. A power user wanting 'my morning in 90 seconds' is forced onto the mouse for everything. InboxView already has per-row Enter-to-file but no way to move BETWEEN rows without clicking. BOLD fix: introduce a small global keymap - g+h/g+c/g+i/g+e style 'go to' chords, 'n' to open a quick task bar, 'c' to capture, '?' for a shortcut cheat-sheet overlay - plus j/k arrow navigation + x multi-select + e (assign topic) / d (set date) / Enter (file) inside Quicks so a 20-item inbox can be cleared in seconds. All client-side event listeners over existing state; respects input-focus guards already present in InboxRow.onKeyDown.

opportunities.title: A cross-topic 'Today / This Week' command center - the one actionable list of everything due
opportunities.area: New aggregated task view + widget (consumes topics[] in Dashboard; complements TodayScheduleWidget.tsx)
opportunities.rationale: Confirmed by grep: there is zero cross-topic task aggregation anywhere. Tasks live only inside TopicTodosWidget, scoped to one topic via ctx.currentTopicId. To see what is due across 8 topics today, Sam must open all 8 pages. TodayScheduleWidget aggregates calendar/deadline EVENTS but is read-only and hard-capped (MAX_TIMED=4, MAX_ALLDAY=3) - you cannot complete or advance anything from it. This directly undercuts the 'your morning in 90 seconds' north star. BOLD fix: a dedicated 'Focus' section (and a home widget) that flattens every topic's items into one keyboard-navigable, fully-actionable list grouped by Overdue / Today / This week / No date, with topic chips, inline stage-advance, deadline edit, and complete - filterable by topic/stage/text. Pure derivation over the existing topics array plus the addTopicItem/onAdvanceStage handlers already in Dashboard.

opportunities.title: Natural-language quick-add with #topic and !date syntax that files straight into a topic
opportunities.area: Capture flow (QuickAddModal.tsx, QuickCapture.tsx, taskParser.ts, voiceRouter.ts)
opportunities.rationale: Every capture path funnels to Quicks first - even QuickAddModal's typed fast-lane (submitTyped) routes to inbox unless voice review is used, and topic assignment is a separate manual triage step later. predictTopic is a fuzzy keyword guess. A power user knows exactly where a task belongs at capture time and wants to say so in one line. BOLD fix: a Todoist/Things-style smart input that parses inline tokens - 'fix login bug #code !fri' creates the task directly in the Code topic with Friday's deadline, bypassing triage entirely; '$' tokens still route to budget. Reuse the existing extractDeadline from taskParser for !date, add a #topic matcher against topics[], live-preview the parsed topic/date chips as you type, and call the existing addTopicItem handler. Fully local, no API/token cost, consistent with the current offline parser philosophy.

opportunities.title: Recurring tasks and saved smart-lists - the automation layer the app is missing
opportunities.area: Task model + new smart-list filters (types.ts TopicItem, TopicTodosWidget.tsx, new filter store)
opportunities.rationale: TopicItem has no recurrence field; only budget RecurringItem repeats. A student's life is full of repeats - 'submit timesheet every Friday', 'water plants Mon/Thu', weekly readings - and right now each must be re-typed. There are also no saved views: every filter (stage pills, sortMode) is ephemeral and per-topic. A power user lives on saved filters ('everything @work due this week', 'overdue across all topics'). BOLD fix: (1) add a lightweight recurrence rule to TopicItem (daily/weekly/every-N-days/weekday-set) with a client-side tick that regenerates the next instance on completion, mirroring the budget recurring pattern already in the codebase; (2) let any filter combination in the new cross-topic Focus view be saved as a named Smart List pinned in the sidebar/palette. Both are self-contained TS over existing storage; no schema/auth/CI changes.

opportunities.title: Make prediction learn: a correction-aware router and inbox-zero batch triage
opportunities.area: Topic prediction + bulk triage (taskParser.ts predictTopic/topicScore, InboxView.tsx, voiceRouter.ts)
opportunities.rationale: predictTopic/topicScore is a fixed keyword heuristic with a hardcoded DOMAIN_KEYWORDS map - it never learns. Every time Sam overrides a wrong prediction in Quicks (touchedRef in InboxRow), that signal is thrown away, so the same mis-prediction recurs forever. Triage is also one-at-a-time: 'Accept all predicted' is the only batch action, and it can only send each item to its predicted topic, not to a chosen one. BOLD fix: persist a small per-topic learned-keyword weighting that increments whenever the user files a Quick into a topic the predictor missed (and decays on overrides), feeding back into topicScore so accuracy compounds with use. Pair it with true batch triage in Quicks - multi-select rows, then assign a single topic/date to all selected - so inbox-zero is a handful of keystrokes. Local-only, layered on the existing scoring and inbox state.

opportunities.title: Make capture a true natural-language command bar, not just an inbox dump
opportunities.area: Quick capture / voiceRouter / taskParser / QuickAddModal
opportunities.rationale: Bozz already owns a moat nobody else has at this price: a 100% offline NLP brain (src/lib/taskParser.ts + src/lib/voiceRouter.ts) that splits multi-task speech, predicts the topic via DOMAIN_KEYWORDS expansion, extracts deadlines, and routes money to budget, with zero API cost or tokens. Akiflow/Sunsama bolt LLM parsing on as a paid, online add-on; Things has no NLP; Notion has none. Yet today this brilliance is buried: typed input in QuickCapture.tsx mostly lands in the inbox and the deadline/topic predictions are only USED in the Quicks triage view, not at the moment of typing. The bold move: promote capture (Ctrl/Cmd+B and the sidebar Quick add) into a Superhuman/Raycast-style command line that parses LIVE as you type and shows an interpreted preview chip ('-> Uni - due Fri - 2 tasks') BEFORE you hit enter, so the user watches Bozz understand them. Add explicit verbs the router already half-handles ('remind me', '/budget', a leading '#topic' override) so power users feel a CLI under their fingers. This directly serves the locked north star (kill the morning context-switch) and the primary user (a 22-38 knowledge worker juggling 4+ tools) by making one keystroke replace opening Notion, Calendar and a budget app. Crucially it stays inside the brand boundary (no AI scheduling, no server LLM) because the parsing is the existing local heuristic engine, just surfaced where it matters. Self-contained React/TS: live-call parseVoiceTasks/routeVoice on each keystroke, render the interpretation, keep TypeScript green.

opportunities.title: Ship a one-screen 'Morning' ritual mode that competitors can't copy without setup
opportunities.area: TodayWidget / HomeView / DailyPlannerView / new MorningView section
opportunities.rationale: The single sharpest differentiator is the literal product promise on the empty home ('your morning, in 90 seconds' in HomeView.tsx) and the auto-aggregating TodayWidget.tsx that already narrates 'X overdue - Y due today - next event 14:00' from real signals. No competitor frames itself this way: Sunsama is a heavy 20-minute daily planning ritual; Akiflow is a command-center; Notion is a blank doc; Things is a list. Bozz should make the 90-second morning an actual guided FLOW, not just a widget. Bold proposal: a dedicated, full-screen 'Start your day' mode (reachable on launch and from Today) that walks three fast cards - (1) here's your day (priorities + first event, straight from TodayWidget's priorities() logic), (2) drag today's 3-5 things from your topics into Today (reusing DailyPlannerView assignment), (3) one tap 'I'm set' that collapses to the dashboard. End-of-day it flips to a 30-second 'shut down' that rolls incomplete items to tomorrow. This turns a passive dashboard into a habit with a beginning and an end, which is exactly what makes Sunsama sticky, but delivered in 90 seconds with zero meeting-import setup. It reinforces 'trust it enough to return tomorrow' from the north star and stays within the no-AI-scheduling boundary because the user does the dragging. Fully self-contained: new section component composing existing widgets/logic; no schema, auth, or network changes.

opportunities.title: Turn Worlds into a shareable, importable template marketplace identity
opportunities.area: WorldsView / lib/worlds / Topic templates
opportunities.rationale: Bozz's Worlds system (src/components/sections/WorldsView.tsx, BozzWorld in types.ts) is genuinely distinctive: a World is theme + ready-made topic pipeline + widget layout + ambient sound, with a polished MiniLayout preview and scoped application (global / new topic / folder). This is Notion-templates-meets-personality, but richer because it carries pipeline stages (Applied/Interviewing/Offer) and a whole page layout, not just a doc. The competitive unlock: Notion's entire growth flywheel is template sharing; Bozz has the better primitive but keeps it a closed bundled gallery (BUNDLED_WORLDS). Bold move: let a user EXPORT their configured topic (stages + widget layout + look, minus private data) as a World JSON, and IMPORT one via paste/file - a self-contained, offline, no-backend 'bring your own template'. A student's 'Dissertation' World or 'Placement applications' World becomes a thing they share in a group chat, and every import is a real populated page in 90 seconds (the north star's 'every shared link shows a real product'). This is aggressive distribution leverage (the #1 constraint per the founder offsite) using machinery that already exists - worldToTopic, worldToFolder, applyWorld. Stays in-bounds: no team features, no server, just client-side serialize/deserialize of the existing BozzWorld shape with a validation guard. Implementable in React/TS against the current worlds lib.

opportunities.title: Make every topic a living 'pipeline' board, beating Notion/Things at non-task workflows
opportunities.area: Topic stages / TopicTodosWidget / topic page
opportunities.rationale: Topics already support arbitrary user-defined stages with done-flags (TopicStage in types.ts) and the Worlds templates exploit this (Job Hunt = Applied/Interviewing/Offer/Rejected). That is a Trello/Notion-database-grade primitive hiding inside a personal dashboard - and it's the thing students actually need that Things and Sunsama refuse to do: applications, dissertation chapters, society events, flat-hunting all move through STAGES, not just todo/done. Right now stages are advanced one-at-a-time via a pill click (DailyPlannerView, TodayWidget). Bold proposal: give each topic page an optional Kanban view of its own stages - drag items across columns, per-stage counts, and a 'time in stage' / stalled indicator (an item sitting in 'Applied' for 14 days surfaces a nudge). This makes Bozz the only morning dashboard where your job hunt or dissertation is a visible board you advance, not a flat list - a concrete reason to pick it over Notion (too heavy) and Things (too flat). It composes from existing TopicItem.stageId + TopicStage data with no model changes, and the 'stalled' nudge reuses completedAt-style timestamps. Self-contained React/TS, keeps TS green, no protected surfaces touched.

opportunities.title: Build a 'Roll-up' weekly Review that auto-writes the recap students would screenshot
opportunities.area: review.ts / ReviewView / topics + budget + habits aggregation
opportunities.rationale: ReviewView.tsx + review.ts already compute weekly spend-by-category and per-topic done/in-progress/upcoming with progress bars - the skeleton of a retrospective competitors charge for or omit (Sunsama's weekly review is a flagship paid ritual; Notion makes you build it manually; Things/Akiflow have nothing). The bold differentiator: turn the Review into an auto-generated, narrated weekly 'wrapped'-style recap that's genuinely satisfying and shareable - 'You finished 12 things across Uni and Job hunt, kept a 6-day gym streak, and spent 41 pounds less than last week.' Pull from data Bozz already has: completed TopicItems by topic, Habit entries/streaks (Habit.entries in types.ts), budget deltas (weeklyStats), and review note history. Add a comparison to the prior week and a single 'momentum' line. This converts a dry stats panel into the emotional payoff loop that makes a calm productivity app sticky and word-of-mouth-able, directly serving the north star's 'trusts it enough to return' and the #1 distribution constraint (people share a good wrapped). Stays firmly within brand boundaries (it's reflection, not AI scheduling) and is pure client-side aggregation over existing state - extend weeklyStats() to cover topics+habits, render a recap card, keep TypeScript green.

opportunities.title: Position Bozz as the calm 'anti-Notion' single home with a frictionless connect story
opportunities.area: AppsView / cold start / onboarding / connectors
opportunities.rationale: Bozz quietly does the integration-heavy thing the primary user (juggling Notion + Google Calendar + a budget tool + Gmail/Outlook) actually wants: real calendar feeds (ical/gcal/caldav), email widgets, Spotify now-playing, Notion quick-open, bank CSV/connect for budget, all surfaced on ONE morning screen. The competitive narrative no rival can honestly claim: Notion is a database you must build; Akiflow/Sunsama are paid command-centers that still bounce you out to other apps; Bozz shows your real calendar, real inbox priorities, real budget and real tasks in the same 90 seconds with a free core. The bold move is to make that narrative undeniable in-product: a guided 'Connect your morning' step in onboarding/AppsView that, after each connect, immediately drops the matching widget onto Home pre-placed and lit with live data (calendar -> Today fills, Gmail -> priority emails appear), so the user SEES the payoff per connection instead of a settings checklist. Combine with the existing coldStart.ts seeding so even before connecting, the dashboard is populated. This sharpens the one knife (kill the morning context-switch), leans on the #1 constraint (first-impression trust), and respects every brand boundary (no team, no AI scheduling, free-core). Implementation reuses ConnectorsBlock/AppsView + HomeView's addWidget; self-contained, no auth/OAuth-secret/schema changes - only wiring existing connect callbacks to widget placement.

priorities.title: Make the real timetable the spine of onboarding (rip out the fake seeded tasks)
priorities.why: Unanimous #1 across CEO, CPO, CTO, COO and the single highest-leverage change in the meeting. Verified in code: coldStart.ts:53-57 seeds fake tasks ('Read this week's lecture notes', 'Revise for the upcoming quiz') the student must delete, and WelcomeColdStart.tsx (invoked via chooseColdStart at Dashboard.tsx:354) never asks for her real timetable. Both halves of the fix already exist and are self-contained: AddFeedForm.tsx (calendar/AddFeedForm.tsx) takes a clean onAdd(feed) callback, already does the webcal://->https rewrite (normalizeFeedUrl) and validates by fetching through fetchFeed. BUILD: after the 'Uni'/'Work' chip, render AddFeedForm as the very next cold-start step with a one-line 'where do I find this?' helper, drop the sample-task seeding, and let her real parsed classes fill Today + the calendar. This converts first-run from 'fake demo' to 'this gets me' and is the literal 'morning in 90 seconds' north star made real. No auth/schema/payments touched.

priorities.title: Unify the 'what's due' stream so the Deadlines widget can finally see imported exams
priorities.why: Unanimous and the credibility fix: a deadline app whose 'Deadlines' widget is structurally blind to her exam dates is broken. Verified: deadlineEntries() in widgets/util.ts:11-29 ingests ONLY topic items; the bridge is one-way (topicDeadlineEvents pushes topics->calendar at Dashboard.tsx:777, nothing reverse), so imported .ics exams never reach priorities(). It's a pure derivation at the source: feedEvents is already memoized (Dashboard.tsx:766) and CalendarEvent already carries allDay + source ('ical'|'deadline'|'note') in types.ts:295-308, so 'deadline-like' (all-day, or title matching due/deadline/exam/submission/coursework) is detectable with zero new model. BUILD: thread feedEvents into WidgetCtx (context.ts only exposes todayEvents today, not the next-7-days stream needed) and have deadlineEntries() also emit deadline-like calendar events. TodayWidget (deadlineEntries at line 71), UpcomingDeadlinesWidget (line 77) and MiniCalendarWidget (line 20) all consume it, so all three get fixed for free. Pairs directly with priority #1: the import is worthless if Deadlines can't see it. TS-safe, no backend.

priorities.title: First-class, topic-free deadline capture that kills the zero-topic dead end
priorities.why: Unanimous #3 and it removes the structural reason a brand-new account stalls. Verified: addTopicItem (Dashboard.tsx:698) requires a topicId, so a deadline can only exist bolted to a topic the user must own first; InboxView.tsx:217 literally tells her 'You have items but no topics yet'. The parser already does the hard part: extractDeadline() (taskParser.ts:300) turns 'stats essay due friday 5pm' into a dated deadline in one line. BUILD: a lightweight 'X is due Y' capture reachable from Quick-add and the Today/Deadlines widgets that works with ZERO topics, defaulting into an auto 'Unfiled'/'Uni' bucket (reuse seedColdStart's topic-creation path or a single ensure-bucket helper), and fix the InboxView wall with inline 'new topic from this Quick'. This makes capture honest about the app's whole reason to exist and lands the deadline somewhere correct (paired with #2). Self-contained over existing setters, no schema change.

experiments.priority: Make the real timetable the spine of onboarding (rip out the fake seeded tasks)

variants.name: Timetable-as-Step-2 (linear flow extension)
variants.approach: Extend the existing welcomePhase state machine so onboarding becomes theme -> identity chip -> REAL timetable, adding one screen rather than rewiring. Concretely: (1) In Dashboard.tsx:118 widen the union to 'theme' | 'coldstart' | 'timetable' | null. (2) Change chooseColdStart (Dashboard.tsx:354) so for the 'uni'/'work' chips it stops setting welcomePhase to null and instead advances to 'timetable', stashing the chosen option in state (the topic + starter home are still seeded for colour/layout/keywords, but the samples are no longer used); 'gym'/'job hunt' chips (no timetable) skip straight to finish. (3) In coldStart.ts delete the sample seeding entirely: in seedColdStart (lines 148-158) set topic.items = [] and remove the fake `samples` arrays from COLD_START_OPTIONS (lines 53-57, 68-72, 83-87, 98-102) plus the `samples` field on the interface. (4) Build a thin WelcomeTimetable.tsx overlay that visually matches WelcomeColdStart (same createPortal, same radial-gradient full-screen shell, same heading style) and embeds the EXISTING AddFeedForm with a neutral dark Theme object passed as `t`, wiring onAdd -> setCalendarFeeds(prev => [...prev, feed]) then setWelcomePhase(null) + save('welcomeComplete', true), plus a one-line 'where do I find this?' helper and a 'Skip / I'll add it later' link. (5) Render it in Dashboard.tsx:1408-1410 next to the existing coldstart conditional. Her real parsed classes then flow through the already-wired refreshFeeds effect (Dashboard.tsx:737-757) into Today + the calendar with zero new data plumbing. Lowest-risk and most faithful to the meeting's literal 'render AddFeedForm as the very next cold-start step'; keeps every screen single-purpose; skipping is graceful (empty topic, no fake tasks).

variants.name: One-screen combined cold-start (chip + timetable together)
variants.approach: Collapse identity + timetable into a single richer WelcomeColdStart screen instead of adding a step, so first-run keeps the same screen count but its headline action becomes pasting the real calendar. Concretely: (1) Restructure WelcomeColdStart.tsx so picking a chip does NOT immediately finish; it sets a local `selected` state and reveals an inline timetable field directly beneath the chip row, reusing AddFeedForm's normalizeFeedUrl + fetchFeed validation (either embed AddFeedForm or lift its submit logic into a compact inline input), with copy that adapts per choice ('Paste your uni timetable link' vs 'Paste your work calendar'). (2) Change the onChoose contract to one richer callback, e.g. onComplete({ option, feed }) where feed may be null if skipped; update chooseColdStart in Dashboard.tsx to seed topic/home from option AND, when feed is present, push it via setCalendarFeeds. (3) Strip the fake samples in coldStart.ts exactly as in variant 1 (topic.items = [], remove sample arrays). (4) The gym/job chips that lack a timetable show a different inline affordance (an 'Add your first real task' field or a plain Continue button) instead of the calendar field, so the form stays contextual. This makes the timetable feel like the natural completion of the identity answer rather than a separate gate and minimises taps on the happy path. Higher build/design effort: the screen now has two states and branches by chip, and AddFeedForm's panel styling must be adapted to the neutral full-screen overlay context. It is the boldest reframing of first-run around the real timetable while preserving the existing theme->one-screen->home rhythm.

variants.name: Timetable-first, identity inferred (chips demoted to fallback)
variants.approach: Invert the order so the very first content step is pasting the REAL timetable, and the identity topic is inferred from it rather than chosen up front. Concretely: (1) After the theme pick (chooseWelcomeMood, Dashboard.tsx:343-348) route welcomePhase straight to a new 'timetable' step instead of 'coldstart', rendering a full-screen overlay that wraps the existing AddFeedForm with a prominent 'where do I find this?' helper and a 'No timetable? Pick a focus instead' link that falls back to the existing chip screen. (2) On successful onAdd: create one neutral 'Uni' topic via worldToTopic(findWorld('study'), 'Uni', 0) for colour/keywords with topic.items = [], push the feed via setCalendarFeeds, apply the 'student' starter home, then finish. (3) Strip the fake samples from coldStart.ts as in the other variants. (4) Optionally surface a tiny inline read-back after the feed parses, e.g. 'Found 12 classes this week' derived from the events fetchFeed already returns before adding, so she sees proof her real data landed before she even reaches home. This is the most opinionated take: the timetable becomes literally step one and home is populated entirely by her real classes, never a demo, maximising the 'this gets me' moment. Trade-offs: it departs most from the established theme->chip->home mental model, makes gym/job-hunt a secondary path, and needs a little extra glue (inferring the topic + the optional 'found N classes' summary) beyond variant 1.

votes.why:
As Maya (first-year), this is the variant that actually makes my real timetable the spine without gambling my first 90 seconds. I confirmed in the code that AddFeedForm already does the whole job: it takes a Theme `t`, runs the real webcal/ics fetch+parse+validation pipeline, hands the feed up via onAdd, and Dashboard's refreshFeeds effect (Dashboard.tsx:755-757) auto-fires whenever calendarFeeds changes — so variant 1 needs ZERO new data plumbing, just a thin overlay reusing existing parts. That's the lowest-risk path to the real goal (ripping out the fake `samples` in coldStart.ts and setting topic.items = []), which all three variants do equally.

experiments.priority: Unify the 'what's due' stream so the Deadlines widget can finally see imported exams

variants.name: Source-level merge: deadlineEntries() ingests deadline-like calendar events
variants.approach: Make the single shared derivation the unification point, so all three consumers (TodayWidget util.ts:71, UpcomingDeadlinesWidget util.ts:77, MiniCalendarWidget util.ts:20) are fixed in one edit. CONTEXT WIRING: add `feedEvents?: CalendarEvent[]` to WidgetCtx (context.ts, alongside the existing `todayEvents` at line 30-31) and thread the already-memoized `feedEvents` (Dashboard.tsx:766) into BOTH ctx construction sites (lines 1445-1453 and 1567-1577) right next to the existing `todayEvents,` prop. SOURCE CHANGE: in deadlineEntries(ctx) (util.ts:11-29), after the existing topic loop, iterate `ctx.feedEvents ?? []`, keep only deadline-like events (`e.allDay === true` OR `/\b(due|deadline|exam|submission|coursework|assignment|hand[- ]?in)\b/i.test(e.title)`), skip `source === 'deadline'` (those are topic deadlines already counted, avoiding a double-count via the topicDeadlineEvents bridge), and push a synthetic DeadlineEntry: `{ item: { id: <stable-number>, text: e.title, status: 'todo', completedAt: null, deadline: dayStart(e.start), dueMin: e.allDay ? null : (minutesFromMidnight(e.start)) }, section: e.sectionId ?? 'calendar', accent: e.color }`. Derive a stable numeric `id` by hashing the event's string id (e.g. a small djb2 over `e.id`) so React keys stay stable and it can never collide with a real topic item lookup. ID-SAFETY: because the synthetic `section` is 'calendar' (a valid SectionId, never a topic id), TodayWidget's TasksSection (TodayWidget.tsx:305 `topics.find(tp => tp.id === p.section)`) finds no topic and silently skips it, so imported exams correctly appear under Priorities/Next-7-days/mini-cal dots but never get pulled into the manual 'My plan' dedup path — no crash, no double surface. Clicking a synthetic entry routes to the calendar via the existing setActiveSection. STRENGTHS: smallest diff, literally the one-source fix the priority describes, automatically correct for any future consumer of deadlineEntries. RISK: deadlineEntries now depends on ctx.feedEvents being passed everywhere it's called — guarded by the `?? []` fallback so older/other call sites stay safe.

variants.name: Adapter layer: a feedDeadlineEntries() helper kept separate, merged per-consumer
variants.approach: Keep deadlineEntries() (util.ts) 100% untouched — it stays the pure topic derivation — and add a SECOND exported helper `feedDeadlineEntries(events: CalendarEvent[]): DeadlineEntry[]` in util.ts that does only the calendar-event side: filter to deadline-like (same `allDay || title-regex` test, excluding `source === 'deadline'`), map each to a synthetic DeadlineEntry exactly as in Variant 1 (hashed numeric id, `section: e.sectionId ?? 'calendar'`, `accent: e.color`, dueMin from start time). Still add `feedEvents?: CalendarEvent[]` to WidgetCtx and thread it from Dashboard.tsx:766 into both ctx sites. Then each of the three consumers composes the two streams at its own call site: UpcomingDeadlinesWidget builds `[...deadlineEntries(ctx), ...feedDeadlineEntries(ctx.feedEvents ?? [])]` before its existing filter/sort (util.ts:77-79); TodayWidget's `priorities()` (TodayWidget.tsx:68-84) iterates the concatenation instead of `deadlineEntries(ctx)`; MiniCalendarWidget's `entries` (line 20) becomes the concatenation. WHY DIFFERENT: this is an additive/composition design rather than mutating the shared primitive — the topic-only derivation keeps a single responsibility and is independently testable, and each widget explicitly opts in (so e.g. a future widget that genuinely wants ONLY topic deadlines isn't silently changed). It also makes the calendar→entry adapter reusable and unit-testable in isolation, and lets a consumer dedupe or weight feed deadlines differently if it ever needs to. STRENGTHS: clean separation of concerns, no behavioural change to existing topic-only path, explicit per-widget control. TRADE-OFF vs Variant 1: three call sites to edit instead of one, and the composition logic is duplicated (mitigated by a tiny shared `allDeadlineEntries(ctx)` wrapper if desired).

variants.name: Upstream bridge: an icalDeadlineEvents() reverse-flow folded into the events pipeline
variants.approach: Fix the asymmetry at the actual root the priority names — the one-way bridge — by making the calendar→deadline flow a first-class event transform in Dashboard.tsx rather than touching widget utils at all for the data shape. Add `icalDeadlineEvents(feedEvents: CalendarEvent[]): CalendarEvent[]` in lib/calendar.ts (sibling to topicDeadlineEvents at calendar.ts:5): it scans feedEvents, picks deadline-like ones (`allDay || title-regex`, excluding `source==='deadline'`), and RE-TAGS each as a normalized deadline-shaped CalendarEvent (`source: 'deadline'`, `allDay: true`, `start: dayStart(original.start)`, preserve color, `sectionId` left undefined so it routes to calendar). In Dashboard.tsx, expose a new memo `deadlineStream = useMemo(() => [...topicDeadlineEvents(topics), ...icalDeadlineEvents(feedEvents)], [topics, feedEvents])` and pass it on WidgetCtx as a new field `deadlineEvents`. Then change the THREE consumers to read deadlines from this unified event stream (windowed to next-7-days where needed) instead of from deadlineEntries: deadlineEntries() in util.ts gets a thin overload/adapter that maps these `source:'deadline'` CalendarEvents back into DeadlineEntry shape so the widgets' existing rendering (which expects DeadlineEntry) is preserved with minimal churn. WHY GENUINELY DIFFERENT: Variants 1 and 2 unify inside the widget layer; this unifies inside the CALENDAR/event layer, so the same normalized deadline stream is simultaneously available to CalendarView (which already consumes feedEvents and topicDeadlineEvents) — meaning imported exams could render with consistent deadline styling on the calendar page too, not just the home widgets. It makes 'a deadline' a single canonical event-stream concept (closing the one-way bridge symmetrically) rather than a widget-only derivation. STRENGTHS: most architecturally complete, single source of truth for 'deadline' across calendar + widgets, future-proof. TRADE-OFF: largest surface area (new memo + new ctx field + adapter in util + rendering touch in three widgets), and the re-tag-to-CalendarEvent-then-map-back round trip is more indirection than Variants 1/2; highest payoff, highest review cost. All TS-safe, no backend, no schema.

experiments.priority: First-class, topic-free deadline capture that kills the zero-topic dead end

variants.name: Auto-Unfiled bucket (ensure-bucket helper)
variants.approach: Make topic-free capture LAND somewhere real by auto-creating a single default bucket on demand, so a deadline never has to be 'bolted to a topic the user owns first'. ADD one helper in Dashboard.tsx, `ensureDefaultTopic(): string`, that returns the id of an existing low-friction bucket or, if none exists, synthesises one by building a topic object via the EXISTING `worldToTopic(findWorld('study'), 'Unfiled', topics.length)` (or seedColdStart's 'uni' path minus the sample items), pushing it through `setTopics`, and returning its id synchronously (build the object first, then setTopics). Then add `captureDeadline(text, deadline)` = `addTopicItem(ensureDefaultTopic(), text, deadline)` and wire it as a NEW path at every capture surface: (1) QuickAddWidget.tsx today calls `addToInbox(v, deadline)` unconditionally — route to `captureDeadline` when a deadline is present (or when topics.length===0); (2) QuickAddModal.tsx `addAll`/`submitTyped`: cards with no topic but WITH a parsed deadline go to `captureDeadline` instead of `inboxItems`, so 'stats essay due friday' lands as a dated task in Unfiled, visible in UpcomingDeadlines/Today immediately; (3) UpcomingDeadlinesWidget.tsx + TodayWidget empty-state 'capture one' buttons open an inline mini-capture calling `captureDeadline` rather than just `setActiveSection('inbox')`. The bucket reuses the same colour/stage machinery so it behaves like any user topic and is renameable later. Smallest-surface, directly removes the structural cause: every dated capture gets a guaranteed home. Self-contained over existing setters; no schema change.

variants.name: Deadlines as a first-class topic-free store (inbox-with-dates surfaced everywhere)
variants.approach: Stop pretending a deadline must live inside a topic: promote the EXISTING inbox `deadline`/`deadlineLabel` fields (already on InboxItem, set by QuickAddModal + parser) into a real, surfaced dataset so a deadline can be a legitimate first-class object with zero topics. Today inbox deadlines are invisible on the home grid; make `deadlineEntries(ctx)` (used by UpcomingDeadlinesWidget.tsx:77 and the Today widget) ALSO include inbox items that have a `deadline`, rendered with a neutral 'Unfiled' accent + small inbox glyph; clicking one routes to Quicks. Then rework the InboxView wall (InboxView.tsx:211-219): instead of 'create a topic in Settings', when a row HAS a deadline render it as a self-sufficient dated card with a complete/done checkbox and an OPTIONAL 'file to topic' affordance, so the deadline is usable WITHOUT owning a topic. Add a 'Deadlines' lens at the top of Quicks (All / Deadlines filter chip) and replace the zero-topics copy with 'these are tracked — file them anytime', not 'you have nowhere to put these'. This treats the inbox as the topic-free deadline store it already half-is, making Quick-add → dated card → visible-on-home work with zero topics and zero auto-created buckets. Bolder reframing; heavier touch on the widget `deadlineEntries` util + InboxView render; still self-contained over `setInbox` + existing fields, no schema change.

variants.name: Inline 'new topic from this Quick' + first-deadline funnel (fix the wall, not the model)
variants.approach: Keep the topic-anchored model but DEMOLISH friction at both ends so the dead end never appears. (A) Fix the InboxView wall directly: replace the dead 'create a topic in Settings → Topics' message (InboxView.tsx:211-219) and add to EVERY InboxRow's ChoicePicker a '+ New topic…' option; selecting it reveals an inline name+colour stub (reusing `makeBlankTopic`) that, on send, creates the topic via a new `onCreateTopic(name): id` callback wired from Dashboard (push to setTopics, return id) and immediately files the item into it — triage and topic creation in one keystroke, no context switch. predictTopic already re-runs per render, so once one topic exists the rest auto-suggest. (B) First-deadline funnel: when `topics.length === 0` and the user captures a dated item from QuickAddWidget / QuickAddModal / the UpcomingDeadlines empty state, show a tiny one-tap 'Where does this go?' sheet offering the 4 COLD_START_OPTIONS chips (Uni / Job / Gym / Work) PLUS 'Unfiled'; picking one runs the existing seedColdStart topic-creation (without the sample tasks) and files the real deadline into it. This turns the very first capture into the topic-creation moment instead of a wall, and lands the deadline somewhere the user actually chose (pairs with priority #2's routing). Most guided of the three; reuses ChoicePicker, makeBlankTopic, COLD_START_OPTIONS, seedColdStart; touches QuickAddModal + InboxView + one small new sheet; self-contained, no schema change.
